skill.name: overdrive
skill.description: Push interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations using modern browser APIs for extraordinary UI effects.
skill.summary: Create extraordinary web interfaces with View Transitions, WebGL, GPU acceleration, and spring physics that make users ask how it was done.
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is a Markdown documentation file containing instructions for AI assistants on creating frontend UI effects. Detected patterns (backticks, code references) are Markdown syntax and inline code examples, not executable commands. No network access, file system operations, external command execution, or data exfiltration patterns exist. The skill teaches standard browser APIs (WebGL, WebGPU, View Transitions, Canvas) for client-side visual effects.

content.user_title: Create extraordinary web interfaces with cutting-edge effects
content.value_statement: Standard web interfaces feel static and predictable. Overdrive transforms them with cinematic transitions, GPU-accelerated visuals, and physics-based animations that surprise and delight users.
actual_capabilities: Implement View Transitions API for morphing page transitions and shared element animations
Create scroll-driven animations using CSS animation-timeline with graceful fallbacks
Build GPU-accelerated visuals with WebGL, WebGPU, and Canvas 2D rendering
Apply spring physics for natural motion with mass, tension, and damping parameters
Optimize performance with virtual scrolling, Web Workers, and OffscreenCanvas
Ensure accessibility with prefers-reduced-motion support and progressive enhancement
limitations: Requires browser automation tools to visually verify and iterate on effects
Some APIs have limited browser support (WebGPU, scroll-driven animations in Firefox)
Must balance technical ambition with design fundamentals and context appropriateness
Performance testing on real mid-range devices is required, not optional

use_cases.title: Creative Portfolio Enhancement
use_cases.description: Add cinematic scroll-driven reveals, particle systems, and shader backgrounds to make a portfolio site memorable and expressive.
use_cases.target_user: Creative developers and designers building personal or agency portfolios

use_cases.title: Enterprise Dashboard Optimization
use_cases.description: Implement virtual scrolling for large data tables, GPU-accelerated charts, and smooth data transitions that maintain 60fps with massive datasets.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend engineers working on data-heavy enterprise applications

use_cases.title: SaaS Product Polish
use_cases.description: Enhance dialogs, forms, and navigation with spring physics, optimistic UI updates, and morphing transitions that feel native and responsive.
use_cases.target_user: Product teams shipping consumer-facing web applications

prompt_templates.title: Basic: Add a Transition Effect
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a View Transition to this page that morphs the list item into the detail view when clicked. Include a fallback for browsers without support.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner-friendly entry into View Transitions API

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Scroll-Driven Hero Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a scroll-driven animation for the hero section where the background parallax scrolls at half speed and text fades in at 30% scroll position. Use CSS-only with a JS fallback.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding scroll-linked animations without JavaScript libraries

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Virtual Table with 100k Rows
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a virtual scrolling table that renders only visible rows from a 100,000 row dataset. Target 60fps scroll performance with sticky headers and row selection.

prompt_templates.title: Expert: WebGL Particle System Background
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an interactive WebGL particle system that responds to mouse movement. Include a lightweight OGL-based implementation with graceful Canvas 2D fallback for unsupported browsers.
prompt_templates.scenario: GPU-accelerated visual effects with progressive enhancement

output_examples.input: Add a cinematic transition when opening a product detail modal from a grid card
output_examples.output: View Transition implementation with shared element morphing: the product image expands from the grid thumbnail to full size, the title slides up from the card position, and the background fades in. Cross-browser fallback uses CSS transforms and opacity transitions.

output_examples.input: Make this data table handle 50k rows without jank
output_examples.output: Virtual scrolling implementation that renders only the visible 20-30 rows, uses CSS contain for layout isolation, and maintains scroll position. Achieves 60fps on mid-range laptops with instant row selection and sorting.

best_practices: Always follow the Context Gathering Protocol - extraordinary effects must match the project's personality and goals
Propose 2-3 different directions with trade-offs before implementation to avoid misaligned effort
Iterate visually with browser automation - effects rarely look right on the first implementation
Target 60fps and respect prefers-reduced-motion as a non-negotiable accessibility requirement
Provide functional fallbacks for all bleeding-edge APIs - the baseline experience must still be good
anti_patterns: Using technical ambition to mask weak design fundamentals - fix design first
Layering multiple competing extraordinary moments - focus creates impact, excess creates noise
Shipping effects that cause jank on mid-range devices or without reduced-motion alternatives
Ignoring the context - particle systems on settings pages are embarrassing, not impressive

faq.question: When should I use this skill versus standard UI implementation?
faq.answer: Use Overdrive when the interface needs to feel extraordinary - marketing pages, creative portfolios, premium product experiences, or any surface where memorable interactions provide competitive advantage. For routine admin panels or content-focused pages, prioritize clarity over spectacle.

faq.question: What browsers do these techniques support?
faq.answer: View Transitions and scroll-driven animations work in Chrome, Edge, and Safari but have limited Firefox support. WebGL works everywhere. WebGPU is Chrome/Edge only. Always provide progressive enhancement - the baseline CSS experience must be functional across all browsers.

faq.question: How do I know if an effect is appropriate for my project?
faq.answer: Follow the skill's preparation protocol: gather design context, understand the project personality, and propose multiple directions to the user. A settings page might need instant optimistic saves, not particle effects. Match the technique to the context.

faq.question: What if the effect causes performance issues?
faq.answer: The skill mandates 60fps targets and reduced-motion support. If dropping below 50fps, simplify the effect. Test on real mid-range devices, not just development machines. Lazy-initialize heavy resources and pause off-screen rendering.

faq.question: Do I need external libraries for these effects?
faq.answer: Many techniques are now native: View Transitions, scroll-driven animations, and @property are CSS-only. For spring physics, libraries like Motion or GSAP help. For WebGL, Three.js or lightweight OGL are options. The skill teaches when native is enough and when libraries add value.

faq.question: How do I verify the effect is working correctly?
faq.answer: Use the skill's verification checklist: the wow test (do users react), the removal test (does absence feel diminished), the device test (smooth on phone and tablet), the accessibility test (beautiful with reduced motion), and the context test (appropriate for this brand).
